title: Lab 10 - Parallel Computing
text:
Evaluate whether a problem can be parallelized.
Vectorize code before reaching for parallelism.
Use joblib.Parallel to parallelize embarrassingly parallel workloads.
Measure and compare serial vs parallel execution time (speedup).

title: Lab 10 - Parallel Computing
text:

title: Lab 10 - Parallel Computing
text:
Problem 1: Identifying parallelizable problems
Take a few minutes to think about what you learned about parallelization.
1a. List three examples of data science / scientific computing tasks that are well-suited to parallel computing. For each one, briefly explain why it is parallelizable (i.e., what makes the sub-tasks independent?).
Answer here.
1b. List one example of a computation that is not easily parallelized, and explain the bottleneck.
Answer here.
1c. Look at the code below. Before running it, predict: will parallelizing this loop give a big speedup? Why or why not?

import numpy as np

def dot_products(args):
    a, b = args
    return np.dot(a, b)

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
pairs = [(rng.standard_normal(1000), rng.standard_normal(1000)) for _ in range(500)]

Your prediction here.
Now time the serial and parallel versions and report the actual speedup.

# Serial
t0 = time.perf_counter()
serial_results = [dot_products(p) for p in pairs]
t_serial = time.perf_counter() - t0

# Parallel — YOUR CODE HERE
# Use Parallel(n_jobs=4)(delayed(dot_products)(p) for p in pairs)
t0 = time.perf_counter()
# ...
t_parallel = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"Serial:   {t_serial*1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"Parallel: {t_parallel*1000:.1f} ms")
# print(f"Speedup:  {t_serial/t_parallel:.2f}x")

Was your prediction correct? Explain the result.

title: Lab 10 - Parallel Computing
text:
Problem 2: Comparing Runtime of Models
Before adding parallelism, make sure your code is already efficient. Parallelizing slow serial code just gives you many slow workers.
2a. The function below generates an n × k matrix where each entry is drawn from a Poisson distribution with mean lambda. It builds the matrix row-by-row using np.vstack, which is inefficient. Write a faster version fun1_fast using a single NumPy call.

import timeit

def fun1(n=100, k=4, lam=4, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    x = None
    for i in range(n):
        row = rng.poisson(lam, k).reshape(1, -1)
        x = row if x is None else np.vstack([x, row])
    return x

def fun1_fast(n=100, k=4, lam=4, seed=0):
    # YOUR CODE HERE — use a single rng.poisson call
    pass

# Verify same shape
assert fun1().shape == fun1_fast().shape, "Shape mismatch!"

# Benchmark
t_slow = timeit.timeit(lambda: fun1(), number=50)
t_fast = timeit.timeit(lambda: fun1_fast(), number=50)
print(f"fun1:      {t_slow*1000/50:.2f} ms per call")
print(f"fun1_fast: {t_fast*1000/50:.2f} ms per call")
print(f"Speedup:   {t_slow/t_fast:.1f}x")

How much faster is your version? Explain briefly why.
2b. The function below finds the row index of the maximum value in each column of a matrix using a Python loop. Write a faster vectorized version using NumPy.

rng = np.random.default_rng(1234)
x = rng.standard_normal((10, 10_000))

def fun2(x):
    result = []
    for j in range(x.shape[1]):
        result.append(np.argmax(x[:, j]))
    return np.array(result)

def fun2_fast(x):
    # YOUR CODE HERE — hint: np.argmax has an axis argument
    pass

# Verify
assert np.array_equal(fun2(x), fun2_fast(x)), "Results differ!"

t_slow = timeit.timeit(lambda: fun2(x), number=20)
t_fast = timeit.timeit(lambda: fun2_fast(x), number=20)
print(f"fun2:      {t_slow*1000/20:.2f} ms per call")
print(f"fun2_fast: {t_fast*1000/20:.2f} ms per call")
print(f"Speedup:   {t_slow/t_fast:.1f}x")

Report the speedup and explain.

title: Lab 10 - Parallel Computing
text:
Problem 3: Parallel XGBoost Hyperparameter Search
Hyperparameter tuning via grid search is a classic embarrassingly parallel task: every combination of hyperparameters can be trained and evaluated completely independently. We will use the CPS dataset from Lab 8 (select 2025 only) and train XGBoost classifiers to predict whether an individual is employed.

Data preparation
Run this cell as-is (no changes needed):

import pandas as pd
import xgboost as xgb
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

cps = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JSC370/JSC370-2026/main/data/cps_clean.csv")
cps = cps[cps['YEAR'].isin([2025])]

# Binary outcome: 1 = employed, 0 = unemployed
cps['employed'] = (cps['worker_cat'] != 'Unemployed').astype(int)

# create race variables
cps_enc = pd.get_dummies(cps, columns=['race_cat'], drop_first=True)

# select features available before observing employment status
feature_cols = ['AGE', 'female', 'married', 'educ_cat',
                'metro_binary', 'us_citizen', 'has_children', 'NCHILD',
                'race_cat_Black', 'race_cat_Other', 'race_cat_White']

X = cps_enc[feature_cols].fillna(0).values.astype(float)
y = cps_enc['employed'].values.astype(int)

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42
)
print(f"Train: {X_train.shape},  Test: {X_test.shape}")
print(f"Employment rate (overall): {y.mean():.3f}")
print(f"Employment rate (train):   {y_train.mean():.3f}")



Hyperparameter grid

Fill in three values for each of these hyperparameters: max_depth, learning_rate, n_estimators, subsample

fdhdzqrn#| eval: false# All combinations — 81 total param_grid = [     {'max_depth': d, 'learning_rate': lr, 'n_estimators': n, 'subsample': ss}     for d in []     for lr in []     for n in []     for ss in [] ] print(f"Total hyperparameter combinations: {len(param_grid)}")
3a. Complete the fit_xgb function below. Use 5-fold cross-validation on the training set to evaluate each hyperparameter combination — this is the correct way to do hyperparameter search, since it avoids overfitting to a single train/test split. The held-out test set is reserved for final evaluation only (see 3c).
Note: set n_jobs=1 on both the XGBoost model and cross_val_score — parallelism comes from running multiple combinations simultaneously via joblib, not from internal threading.

def fit_xgb(params):
    """Evaluate one hyperparameter combination via 5-fold CV."""
    # YOUR CODE HERE
    # 1. Build an XGBClassifier with the given params, n_jobs=1,
    #    eval_metric='logloss', random_state=42, verbosity=0
    # 2. Run cross_val_score with cv=5, scoring='roc_auc', n_jobs=1 on (X_train, y_train)
    # 3. Return (params, scores.mean())
    pass

# Serial grid search
t0 = time.time()
serial_results = [fit_xgb(p) for p in param_grid]
time_serial = time.time() - t0
print(f"Serial grid search: {time_serial:.2f}s  ({len(param_grid)} combinations)")

3b. Parallelize the grid search using joblib.Parallel with 4 workers. Measure the wall-clock time and compute the speedup.

t0 = time.time()
# YOUR CODE HERE
# Use: Parallel(n_jobs=4)(delayed(fit_xgb)(p) for p in param_grid)
parallel_results = None
time_parallel = time.time() - t0

print(f"Parallel grid search (4 workers): {time_parallel:.2f}s")
print(f"Speedup: {time_serial / time_parallel:.2f}x")

3c. Verify the serial and parallel CV AUC scores match, report the best hyperparameter combination, then refit the best model on the full training set and evaluate once on the held-out test set.

# YOUR CODE HERE
# 1. Extract mean CV AUC lists from serial_results and parallel_results
# 2. Assert they are close with np.allclose(..., atol=1e-6)
# 3. Find the entry in parallel_results with the highest mean CV AUC
# 4. Refit an XGBClassifier with best params on X_train, evaluate on X_test
#    using roc_auc_score — this is the final unbiased test performance

3d. Experiment with 1, 2, 4, and multiprocessing.cpu_count() workers. Print a timing table and plot speedup vs number of workers.

worker_counts = [1, 2, 4, multiprocessing.cpu_count() - 1]
times = {}

for n_workers in worker_counts:
    t0 = time.time()
    Parallel(n_jobs=n_workers)(delayed(fit_xgb)(p) for p in param_grid)
    times[n_workers] = time.time() - t0
    print(f"workers={n_workers}: {times[n_workers]:.2f}s  "
          f"speedup={time_serial / times[n_workers]:.2f}x")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.plot(list(times.keys()), [time_serial / t for t in times.values()], marker='o')
ax.axhline(1, color='gray', linestyle='--', label='serial baseline')
ax.set_xlabel("Number of workers")
ax.set_ylabel("Speedup")
ax.set_title(f"Parallel speedup: XGBoost grid search ({len(param_grid)} combos)")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Does speedup scale linearly with the number of workers? Why or why not? What overhead is present even in an embarrassingly parallel task like this?
